title: Multi-Part Prompts
concept: Composing a prompt from instruction blocks, context blocks, and few-shot blocks as independent modules.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, how did we guarantee that an LLM's output matches a strict JSON format?

options.text: By asking the model nicely in the prompt to use valid JSON
By restricting the output tokens to match a defined JSON schema
By running the output through a python script to fix typos afterwards

exercises.explain: Schema-constrained generation uses tools like JSON Schema or grammars to restrict the LLM's raw output token-by-token, ensuring it always returns syntactically valid data.

cards.title: Lost in the Text Dump
cards.body: Have you ever written a long message to a friend with multiple questions, only for them to answer just the last one? AI does the same thing when we dump too much unorganized text on it!

cards.title: What is a Multi-Part Prompt?
cards.body: A multi-part prompt separates instructions, background context, and examples into clean, modular blocks. By organizing these pieces systematically, we prevent the model from getting confused between instructions and the data it needs to process.

cards.title: The Bento Box Prompt
cards.body: Think of a multi-part prompt like a bento box. Instead of mixing your rice, teriyaki chicken, and pickled veggies into a messy pile, you keep them in distinct compartments so the flavors don't bleed together.

cards.title: A Structured Assembly
cards.body: Instead of writing one giant paragraph, you assemble your prompt using templates: first the [System Instruction], then the [Context Document] wrapped in XML tags, and finally the [User Query].

cards.title: More Words Doesn't Mean More Clarity
cards.body: Many believe that writing longer, messy paragraphs helps the AI understand 'nuance'. In reality, clear structural boundaries and modular blocks prevent the model from ignoring instructions or mixing up data.

cards.title: Modular vs. Tangled
cards.body: Separating your prompt elements makes it easy for the AI to parse, whereas mixing them together creates confusion.

left.text: Instructions, Context, and Examples separated by clear headers.

right.text: Instructions mixed in with user data in one long block of text.

exercises.prompt: Why do we wrap context or examples in XML tags when building multi-part prompts?

options.text: To make the prompt look like a web page
To create clear boundaries between instructions and input data
To bypass the model's safety guardrails
To compress the prompt and save token costs

exercises.explain: XML tags (like <context></context>) act as clear boundary markers, telling the LLM exactly where the background information starts and ends.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Structuring prompts into independent modules makes it much easier to test and update individual parts of your application.
exercises.explain: Keeping prompt parts modular allows you to update the instructions (e.g., change the tone) without touching the context or user inputs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how we organize a multi-part prompt template:
exercises.sentence: To make prompts easier to maintain, we split them into modular blocks like context and instructions.
exercises.explain: Building prompts using modular blocks allows you to easily swap out datasets, instructions, or examples without rewriting the entire prompt template.

exercises.prompt: Which of these prompts uses a modular, multi-part structure?

options.text: Hey AI, please read this paragraph about frogs: 'Frogs are amphibians.' Now tell me what color they are.
Can you summarize this text for me? Text: The solar system is old. Summarize it in 2 words.

[Instruction] Summarize the text below.
[Context]
Our company was founded in 1999 to sell shoes online.
[Query] What does the company do?

exercises.explain: Option C demonstrates a structured, multi-part prompt with clear modular blocks (instructions, context, and query) separated by clean delimiters. Options A and B are unstructured, conversational queries.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer support bot that needs to analyze a transcript using a specific company handbook. What is the best way to structure this complex prompt?

options.text: Combining instructions and data into one long, unstructured paragraph
Splitting the prompt into clear, modular sections like instructions, context, and examples
Letting the user write the system instructions directly in the prompt
Ignoring formatting tags and letting the LLM guess the structure

exercises.explain: Dividing your prompt into modular parts (System Instructions, External Context, Examples, and User Input) keeps the LLM from getting confused about where instructions end and data begins. It also makes your code clean and reusable!
